steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The team concludes that exposure drives the ability to distinguish familiar from unfamiliar languages. A supporting finding must show that greater exposure produces stronger distinction ability.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "The difference between the pattern of brain activity a dog showed when hearing the language it was accustomed to and the pattern of brain activity it showed when hearing the language it was not accustomed to was greatest among older dogs.": Older dogs have accumulated more exposure over time, and their larger brain-activity gap directly links exposure to sharper distinction ability. ✓
  • "The similarity between the pattern of brain activity a dog showed in response to hearing the scrambled recording and the pattern of brain activity it showed in response to hearing the language it was not accustomed to was greatest among older dogs.": Scrambled non-speech is not an unfamiliar language, so this does not test the familiar-vs-unfamiliar distinction the conclusion describes. ✗
  • "Although the dogs' general hearing sensitivity declined with age, dogs of all ages showed more brain activity in response to hearing the language they were accustomed to than in response to hearing the other language.": Uniform results across ages would suggest exposure has no effect, undercutting rather than supporting the team's claim. ✗
  • "Dogs showed a different pattern of brain activity when hearing the language they were accustomed to than when hearing the scrambled recording, and the difference in brain activity increased with the age of the dog scanned.": Again involves scrambled non-speech rather than the language-vs-language comparison needed. ✗
That matches the correct answer — A is correct.
